question: How does the conceptual site layout in Figure 1.2-1 relate to the DHRS schematic in Figure 5.4-7 in terms of the reactor pool?

nuscale_ch01_0043.title: Ultimate Heat Sink
nuscale_ch01_0043.summary: The Ultimate Heat Sink for the NuScale plant is a large, stainless steel-lined, reinforced concrete pool located below grade, comprising the reactor, refueling, and spent fuel pool areas. During normal operations, heat is removed via a cooling system and external heat sink, while in a loss of all AC power, decay heat is passively transferred to the pool, which has sufficient water inventory to ensure continued cooling through heat-up and boiling.

nuscale_ch01_0016.title: Principal Site Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0016.summary: This section provides a general overview of the NuScale plant design, outlining key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling, and waste management. It explains that each Combined License (COL) applicant must reference the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) and supplement it with site-specific information, while distinguishing between certified design elements and conceptual design information (CDI) for context and regulatory compliance.

nuscale_ch01_0068.title: Electrical and Instrumentation and Control Drawings
nuscale_ch01_0068.summary: This section describes the inclusion of simplified electrical, instrumentation, and control (I&C) drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information alongside the written text. It references tables and figures listing and explaining the symbols used in these diagrams, and notes that applicants referencing the NuScale design must provide additional site-specific diagrams and legends as needed.

nuscale_ch01_0015.title: General Plant Description
nuscale_ch01_0015.summary: This section provides a summary of the NuScale plant’s overall design, including key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling, and waste management. It explains the distinction between certified and site-specific design elements, the use of representative conceptual design information (CDI), and outlines how each COL Applicant must reference and supplement the NuScale FSAR with site-specific details. Security-related information is specially marked and withheld according to regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0035.title: Fuel Handling and Reactor Maintenance Areas
nuscale_ch01_0035.summary: The fuel handling and reactor maintenance areas are located at the west end of the Reactor Building (RXB) and include the spent fuel pool (SFP), refueling pool, and dry dock, with dedicated spaces and equipment for fuel handling and maintenance activities. These areas are designed for safe transport, inspection, and temporary storage of both new and spent fuel assemblies, while providing radiation protection for personnel.

nuscale_ch01_0069.title: Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams
nuscale_ch01_0069.summary: This section describes the use of simplified electrical, instrumentation and control (I&C), and mechanical drawings in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report to visually clarify design information. It lists tables and figures that provide functional diagrams, one-line diagrams, and legends for symbols used in these drawings, and notes that site-specific diagrams and legends will be provided by COL applicants referencing the NuScale design certification.

nuscale_ch01_0669.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.4 Specific CriterionInstrument Spans and Setpoints use RG 1.105
nuscale_ch01_0669.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, addressing events such as changes in feedwater temperature, flow, steam flow, and inadvertent system operations. It confirms that NuScale meets specific criteria for system pressure, critical heat flux, event classification, and instrument setpoints, aligning with NRC guidance and ensuring plant safety under these conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0025.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0025.summary: This section details the materials used in the construction of the pressurizer and its components within the NuScale reactor coolant system, specifying alloys and standards for each part to ensure corrosion resistance and compatibility. It references relevant sections of the BPVC (Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code) and lists the specific alloys and material specifications for the baffle plate, heater bundles, cover plate, bolting, and heater sheaths.

nuscale_ch01_0673.title: DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0: Decrease in Feedwater Temperature, Increase in Feedwater Flow, Increase in Steam Flow, and Inadvertent Opening of the Turbine Bypass System or Inadvertent Operation of the Decay Heat Removal SystemII.3 Analytical ParametersCore Burnup
nuscale_ch01_0673.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, which address events such as decreases in feedwater temperature, increases in feedwater or steam flow, and inadvertent operation of the turbine bypass or decay heat removal systems. It confirms that analytical parameters—including initial power level, conservative scram, core burnup, and setpoint inaccuracies—meet regulatory requirements, with no exceptions noted.

nuscale_ch05_0022.title: Decay Heat Removal System
nuscale_ch05_0022.summary: The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature designed to remove residual and core decay heat from the reactor after shutdown, ensuring safe cooling without external power during non-LOCA events. It maintains the reactor coolant system (RCS) temperature below 420°F within 36 hours, preventing fuel or pressure boundary limits from being exceeded, and operates independently of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS), though both can function together if needed. The DHRS's reliability and risk significance are systematically evaluated, and it meets applicable regulatory and quality requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes key plant features of special interest in the NuScale Power Plant, including the Central Utility Building (CUB) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs), which house essential but nonsafety-related utility systems and backup generators. It also highlights human factors considerations, emphasizing fail-safe design, automation, digital controls, and workload management to minimize human error and meet regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0036.title: Refueling Operations
nuscale_ch01_0036.summary: Refueling operations for a NuScale Power Module (NPM) are conducted independently from other modules and involve moving the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling pool using a reactor building crane (RBC). The process includes disassembly for inspection, maintenance, and refueling, followed by reassembly and return to the operating bay, where startup tests are performed before the module is brought back online.

nuscale_ch01_0032.title: Radioactive Waste Management System
nuscale_ch01_0032.summary: The section describes the radioactive waste management system at the NuScale Power Plant, noting that liquid, gaseous, and solid waste systems, as well as radiation monitoring, are detailed in Chapter 11. It also outlines the general arrangement of major structures, focusing on the centrally located, reinforced concrete Reactor Building (RXB), which houses the nuclear power modules and is designed for safety, radiation protection, and resistance to various hazards.

nuscale_ch01_0037.title: Control Building
nuscale_ch01_0037.summary: This section describes the refueling operations for a NuScale Power Module (NPM), which are conducted independently for each module. The process involves moving the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling area, disassembling it for inspection, maintenance, and refueling, and then reassembling and returning it to service after successful testing and inspection.

nuscale_ch01_0033.title: General Arrangement of Major Structures and Equipment
nuscale_ch01_0033.summary: This section describes the general arrangement of major structures and equipment at a NuScale Power Plant, focusing on the central placement and design of the Reactor Building (RXB). The RXB is a reinforced concrete, Seismic Category I structure designed for safety and radiation protection, housing the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) in a common reactor pool arranged in two rows. The section also references detailed figures and chapters for further information on radioactive waste management and building layouts.
